sourceMovie.title: Thanksgiving
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: People revisit Thanksgiving for the same reason it broke through in 2023: clear stakes, memorable faces, and a mystery rhythm that doesn't stall in the middle. Eli Roth keeps the ensemble pointed in one direction. The horror and mystery blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Scream
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind Scream (1996) clearly understood what makes horror work — the same quality that keeps Thanksgiving rewatchable. Expect the same dark rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

recommendations.title: Scream 2
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Scream 2 (1997) is the kind of film Thanksgiving fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The horror overlap is real, but it's the dark emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

recommendations.title: Black Christmas
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Black Christmas shares Thanksgiving's gift for horror — the 1974 film delivers mystery without overstaying its welcome. Consider it required viewing once you've finished rewatching your first pick.

recommendations.title: I Am Not a Serial Killer
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Much of what makes Thanksgiving compelling — the horror, the mystery — shows up in I Am Not a Serial Killer (2016) in a slightly different form. Both films understand that dark storytelling works best when it trusts the audience.

recommendations.title: Intruder
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The horror energy of Thanksgiving is alive in Intruder (1989), filtered through a different story but the same emotional register. It earns its place on this list with a dark payoff that rivals the original pick.

recommendations.title: Scream VI
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Scream VI (2023) operates in the same horror as Thanksgiving, but with its own distinct voice and pacing. The dark payoff will feel satisfyingly familiar without retreading the same ground.

recommendations.title: Halloween II
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Halloween II (1981) belongs to the same conversation as Thanksgiving — both anchor their horror in thriller emotion that doesn't let go. The dark undercurrent connects them more than any plot summary would suggest.

recommendations.title: Trick 'r Treat
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Thanksgiving's horror stayed with you, Trick 'r Treat (2007) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The comedy through-line is the real common thread between them.

recommendations.title: Murder on the Orient Express
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Where Thanksgiving excels in mystery, Murder on the Orient Express (1974) doubles down on drama to comparable effect. The tonal similarities run deep enough that watching both back-to-back feels intentional.

recommendations.title: Critters
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Critters (1986) is the natural next step after Thanksgiving: same horror DNA, different perspective. The dark streak and comedy elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

faq.question: What should I watch after Thanksgiving?
faq.answer: Try Scream, Scream 2, Black Christmas—each shares DNA with Thanksgiving while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Thanksgiving?
faq.answer: Thanksgiving (2023) is best described as Horror, Mystery. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Thanksgiving part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Thanksgiving Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
